subject: ap-calculus-bc
title: Parametric Equations — Particle Motion
stimulus: For $0 \leq t \leq \pi$, a particle is moving along a curve so that its position at time $t$ is $(x(t),\, y(t))$. It is given that $\dfrac{dx}{dt} = \sin(t^2)$ and $y(t) = \cos(2t) + 2\sin(t)$. At time $t = 0$, the particle is at position $(1, 2)$.

parts.prompt: Find the acceleration vector of the particle at time $t = 1$. Show the setup for your calculations.
parts.scoring_notes: Acceleration vector $= \left(\dfrac{d^2x}{dt^2},\, \dfrac{d^2y}{dt^2}\right)$ evaluated at $t = 1$. $\dfrac{d^2x}{dt^2} = \dfrac{d}{dt}[\sin(t^2)] = 2t\cos(t^2)$; at $t=1$: $2\cos(1)$. $\dfrac{dy}{dt} = -2\sin(2t) + 2\cos(t)$; $\dfrac{d^2y}{dt^2} = -4\cos(2t) - 2\sin(t)$; at $t=1$: $-4\cos(2) - 2\sin(1)$. Acceleration vector $\approx (1.080,\, -3.328)$.

sample_responses.commentary: Correctly applies the chain rule to differentiate $\sin(t^2)$: derivative of outer function $\cos(t^2)$ times derivative of inner function $2t$.
Forgot the chain rule factor $2t$ when differentiating $\sin(t^2)$. The correct derivative is $2t\cos(t^2)$, not $\cos(t^2)$.

common_traps: Forgetting the chain rule when differentiating $\sin(t^2)$

sample_responses.commentary: Differentiates $y(t) = \cos(2t) + 2\sin(t)$ twice correctly, applying the chain rule on $\cos(2t)$ to get the factor of $-4$.
Sign error: differentiating $-2\sin(2t)$ gives $-4\cos(2t)$, not $+4\cos(2t)$. The leading coefficient must be negative.

common_traps: Differentiating only once to get velocity instead of twice for acceleration

parts.prompt: For $0 \leq t \leq \pi$, find the first time $t$ at which the speed of the particle is $1.5$. Show the work that leads to your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: Speed $= \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{dx}{dt}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{dy}{dt}\right)^2} = \sqrt{\sin^2(t^2) + (-2\sin(2t) + 2\cos(t))^2}$. Set equal to $1.5$ and solve with a calculator. The first time is $t \approx 0.443$.

scoring_points.description: Correct speed expression set equal to $1.5$: $\sqrt{\sin^2(t^2) + (-2\sin(2t) + 2\cos(t))^2} = 1.5$.

sample_responses.commentary: Correctly sets up the speed formula with both components under the radical and sets it equal to $1.5$.
Speed requires the sum of squares of the velocity components, not the sum of the components themselves. Each term under the radical must be squared.

common_traps: Using velocity components instead of their squares under the radical

sample_responses.response: Using a calculator to solve $\sqrt{\sin^2(t^2) + (-2\sin(2t)+2\cos(t))^2} = 1.5$ on $[0,\pi]$, the first solution is $t \approx 0.443$.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly uses technology to find the smallest positive $t$ where speed equals $1.5$.

sample_responses.response: The equation gives $t \approx 1.247$ as the first solution.
sample_responses.commentary: Reports a later zero rather than the first. The problem requires the first time $t$ at which speed equals $1.5$, which occurs at $t \approx 0.443$.

common_traps: Reporting a later value of $t$ instead of the first

parts.prompt: Find the slope of the line tangent to the path of the particle at time $t = 1$. Find the $x$-coordinate of the position of the particle at time $t = 1$. Show the work that leads to your answers.

scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: slope $= \dfrac{dy/dt}{dx/dt}\bigg|_{t=1} = \dfrac{-2\sin(2)+2\cos(1)}{\sin(1)}$ with correct numerator and denominator

sample_responses.commentary: Correctly forms the ratio of $dy/dt$ over $dx/dt$ and substitutes $t=1$ into each component.
Inverted the ratio. The slope of the curve in the $xy$-plane is $dy/dx = (dy/dt)\div(dx/dt)$, not $(dx/dt)\div(dy/dt)$.
Evaluates numerator and denominator numerically and divides to get the correct slope value.
Likely computed $\sin(1)\div(-2\sin(2)+2\cos(1))$ (inverted ratio) or made a calculator error. The correct slope is approximately $-2.141$.
Correctly uses the FTC with the given initial condition $x(0)=1$ to recover $x(1)$ by integrating $dx/dt$ from $0$ to $1$.
Forgot to add the initial $x$-value of $1$. The particle starts at $(1,2)$ at $t=0$, so $x(1) = 1 + \int_0^1 \sin(t^2)\,dt \approx 1.310$.

common_traps: Forgetting to add the initial $x$-value of $1$

parts.prompt: Find the total distance traveled by the particle over the time interval $0 \leq t \leq \pi$. Show the setup for your calculations.

scoring_points.description: Correct integral setup for arc length: $\int_0^{\pi} \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{dx}{dt}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{dy}{dt}\right)^2}\,dt = \int_0^{\pi}\sqrt{\sin^2(t^2) + (-2\sin(2t)+2\cos(t))^2}\,dt$.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: $\displaystyle\int_0^{\pi}\sqrt{(dx/dt)^2 + (dy/dt)^2}\,dt = \int_0^{\pi}\sqrt{\sin^2(t^2)+(-2\sin(2t)+2\cos(t))^2}\,dt$ with correct limits and components

sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies total distance as the arc length integral with limits $0$ to $\pi$ and substitutes the correct velocity components.
Used incorrect limits of integration. The problem specifies the time interval $0 \leq t \leq \pi$, not $0$ to $1$.

common_traps: Omitting one component under the radical

sample_responses.commentary: Correctly uses technology to evaluate the arc length integral over $[0,\pi]$.
Calculator was likely in degree mode instead of radian mode. All trigonometric expressions use radian measure; the correct answer is approximately $4.706$.

common_traps: Arithmetic error or calculator mode error (degrees vs. radians)
